ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken skin food product class; no specific deep-fried crusted variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated crusted/battered chicken skin code. A00GJ (poultry skin, fried) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for crusted fried chicken skin as a food product.

preparation_method: coated with flour or cornstarch crust
main_ingredients: cooking oil (palm oil or vegetable oil)

serving.description: 1 small plate of crusted chicken skin
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crusted chicken skin is approximately 100 g, equivalent to one small plate or snack portion as commonly served at hawker stalls and food delivery outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, skin only, raw (NDB No. 05025) and Chicken, skin only, fried (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'crusted chicken skin' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken skin (NDB 05025 / 05026) with adjustment for seasoning and coating contribution to carbohydrate and sodium. Energy, protein, fat, and saturated fat are anchored to USDA fried chicken skin data. Carbohydrate and sodium reflect the seasoned, coated preparation. Input values (energy 550 kcal, protein 15 g, fat 50 g, carbohydrate 10 g, sodium 450 mg, saturated fat 18 g) are consistent with USDA fried chicken skin range and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crusted fried chicken skin. The GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on the analogous food category of battered/coated fried poultry products with a wheat flour or cornstarch coating, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar coated fried foods (GI range 45–65). The carbohydrate content is low (10 g/100g) from the coating, so the glycemic load per 100 g serving is 5.5 (low). GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 10) / 100 = 5.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100 g combined with very high saturated fat 18 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid crusted chicken skin: sodium at 450 mg/100 g approaches the per-meal limit for a sodium-restricted diet, and the extremely high saturated fat load (18 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure over time; avoid additional dipping sauces and limit portion to an occasional small taste only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high total fat (50 g/100 g) and saturated fat (18 g/100 g) impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid crusted chicken skin: the extremely high fat content (50 g/100 g) from deep-frying significantly impairs insulin sensitivity, and the saturated fat load (18 g/100 g) is clinically significant for long-term glycaemic control and cardiovascular co-morbidity management; the refined flour coating adds rapidly digestible carbohydrate with negligible fibre offset.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 18 g/100 g, trans fat 0.5 g/100 g, cholesterol 100 mg/100 g, and deep-frying AGE formation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crusted chicken skin is contraindicated for individuals with cardiovascular disease: saturated fat at 18 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.5 g/100 g directly raise LDL cholesterol, cholesterol at 100 mg/100 g adds dietary cholesterol burden, and deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote endothelial inflammation and atherosclerosis progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying AGE formation and saturated fat 18 g/100 g far exceeding the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly avoid crusted chicken skin: deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and saturated fat at 18 g/100 g (from chicken skin and palm oil) far exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold associated with increased inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crusted Chicken Skin is a deep-fried snack food made from chicken skin coated in a seasoned wheat flour or cornstarch batter and fried in oil (commonly palm oil or vegetable oil) until golden brown and very crispy. It is popular as a street food and hawker snack in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often served as a side dish or standalone snack with dipping sauces. The texture is extremely crunchy on the outside with a rich, fatty interior from the rendered chicken skin. The flavour is savoury and umami-forward, seasoned with salt, garlic powder, and onion powder. Nutritionally, crusted chicken skin is calorie-dense at approximately 550 kcal per 100 g, with very high fat content (50 g/100 g) dominated by saturated fat (18 g/100 g), moderate protein (15 g/100 g) from the skin, and low carbohydrate (10 g/100 g) from the coating. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg/100 g. Cholesterol is 100 mg/100 g. The food is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat load, sodium content, trans fat, and AGE formation from deep-frying. It contains gluten from the wheat flour coating and is not suitable for gluten-free diets. It is halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified chicken and oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried chicken skin?
Is chicken skin high in cholesterol?
What is crispy chicken skin made of?
Is fried chicken skin bad for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crusted chicken skin exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken skin data and are consistent with the input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Medium confidence is assigned because the adaptation is defensible and the values fall within the expected range for this food type, but no direct approved-source measurement of this specific preparation exists.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried crusted chicken skin preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — chicken skin contains no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central fried chicken skin entry). Input cultural tags ('Thai', 'Seafood Dish', 'Prawn') were disregarded as clearly erroneous for this food; country_of_origin is Singapore and cuisine is Singaporean/Southeast Asian. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean dish requirements: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. GI estimated at 55 (low) from analogous coated fried poultry products per University of Sydney GI Database; GL per 100 g serving = 5.5 (low). Halal status assumed based on typical preparation; kosher status not confirmed.
